title: Spinach & Chickpea Curry

overview.description: A North Indian-style curry of chickpeas cooked in a richly spiced spinach and tomato masala.

ingredients: 2 x 400g tins chickpeas, drained and rinsed
method: If using fresh spinach, blanch it in boiling water for 1 minute.
Drain and cool under cold water.
Squeeze out as much water as possible.
Blend the spinach into a coarse purée, adding a little water if necessary.
Heat the oil in a large saucepan over medium heat.
Add the cumin seeds and fry until aromatic.
Add the onion.
Cook until softened and golden.
Add the garlic, ginger and green chilli if using.
Cook for 1–2 minutes until fragrant.
Add the chopped tomatoes, turmeric, ground coriander, ground cumin, Kashmiri chilli powder and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes have broken down and the masala becomes thick and glossy.
Add the spinach purée.
Cook for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add the chickpeas and water.
Stir well and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook for 15–20 minutes so the chickpeas absorb the flavour of the masala.
Add the garam masala.
Finish with the lemon juice and fresh coriander.
Taste and adjust the seasoning before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Palak chana combines chickpeas with palak, meaning spinach. It belongs to the broad family of Indian dishes where leafy greens are cooked into a richly spiced masala.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to turn leafy greens into a rich curry base and how chickpeas absorb flavour during a gentle simmer.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A cheap, nutritious Pantry Monday curry that makes excellent use of chickpeas and spinach while offering a completely different texture from dal and a different flavour profile from saag paneer.
chapman-tips: Squeeze the spinach very thoroughly after blanching so the curry does not become watery.
Don't rush the onion and tomato masala; this is where much of the flavour develops.
For a thicker curry, mash a small portion of the chickpeas into the sauce.
For a smoother curry, blend the spinach completely before adding it.
A little lemon juice at the end is important because spinach and chickpeas both benefit from acidity.
Frozen spinach works very well here and is particularly convenient for Pantry Monday.
For a milder family version, omit the green chilli and use a mild Kashmiri chilli powder.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Allow to cool completely before freezing in portions.
notes.leftovers: Reheats very well. The curry may thicken in the refrigerator, so add a splash of water when reheating.
